text: It's a beautiful day here in South Florida and today I'm going to go out and see if I can collect everything I need to make dinner tonight. So come with me. First up, we're going to harvest some breadfruit. For those of you unfamiliar with this fruit, it actually acts more like a potato. You can roast, bake, boil, or fry it. But when the fruits are small and green like this one here, my favorite way to prepare them is breadfruit mash, which is kind of like mashed potatoes. After that, I went over to the quail coop and collected some fresh eggs. It's been a couple days since I did this, so there were quite a few. Next, I headed over to my mango tree to pick a couple of them. And it wasn't long before I had a whole bunch. After that, I picked some fresh key limes, as well as some fresh calamans and limes. While the outside of these fruits look pretty similar, the flavor and the color on the inside is completely different. Then I picked some leafy greens from the garden for our salad tonight, including some longevity spinach, some ciso spinach, and cranberry habiscus leaves, which give a nice little cranberry flavor. And last but not least, I harvested some Cuban oregano and rosemary, and now it's time to go find the main course. For the main course, we're going to be targeting an invasive type of freshwater fish here in South Florida, known as the Mayan Cyclad. These fish are aggressive feeders and will eat just about anything, but an earthworm is always a pretty safe bet. In total, today we ended up getting three nice ones. Back at the house, I quickly went ahead and filleted the fish, and now it's time to get to cooking. First, I started by making the breadfruit mash. This is super simple. You just want to remove the skin and the core of the breadfruit, and then boil it until it's tender. Mash it up with a fork, add butter, salt, pepper, and a little bit of chopped up rosemary. Next, I chopped up all our leafy greens from the garden, and made a quick salad dressing using a little calamundan juice, some fish sauce, red chili flakes, and a little bit of ginger. Next, to fry our fish, I went ahead and coated it in flour, then dipped it in an egg wash using the quail eggs we harvested earlier, then coated it with seasoned breadcrumbs, and fried it until a crispy golden brown. And for our dipping sauce, I chopped up our rosemary and oregano, and mixed it with yogurt, a little bit of olive oil, and some salt and pepper. And last but not least, I went ahead and juiced our keylimes to make a delicious key lime pie from scratch, and because I had some extra calamundan juice, I decided to make a calamundan pie as well. Once everything was done, I just plate it it up, and there we have it. Dinner is served. It looks good, and it smells good, but now it's time to see how it tastes. First, I'm going to try a little bit of the salad. The dressing gives it a nice little sour vinaigrette taste with a nice little kick of spice at the end, but now it's time to try the fish with our sauce. Mmm, I would put this up against tilapia any day. And breadfruit mash just never gets old. As the fruits get older, they'll get a bit more starchy, and so it'll look more like mashed potatoes, but even like this, they're delicious. And who doesn't love a ripe Julie mango? And to wash it all down, we have our calamundan juice. And now that I'm done with dinner, it's time to try the desserts. First up, I'm going to try the key lime pie out of this world delicious. Time to see how it compares to the calamundan pie. They both are insanely delicious, but I would have to say I'd go with the key lime pie over the calamundan, because you can definitely taste the different flavor profiles, and I just really prefer the key lime. Until next time, get outside and see what you can find.
